explanation: SET NOCOUNT is explicitly disabled, causing row-count messages to be sent to callers.
SET XACT_ABORT is disabled even though the procedure uses explicit transactions.
SELECT * from Sales.Orders returns every current and future column unnecessarily.
TOP (10) has no ORDER BY, so the selected orders are arbitrary.
OrderID is the primary key of Sales.Orders, so DISTINCT does not remove duplicates.
NOLOCK can return uncommitted, missing, or duplicated Sales.Orders rows.
YEAR is applied to OrderDate in the predicate, preventing a simple date-range seek.
The int CustomerID column is compared with the nvarchar @CustomerIdText variable, requiring an implicit conversion.
The optional-parameter OR predicate can prevent efficient seeking and produce poor cardinality estimates.
The LIKE pattern starts with %, preventing a normal index seek on Comments.
ISNULL wraps CustomerID in the predicate, making the condition non-sargable.
Arithmetic is applied to OrderID in the predicate, preventing direct index matching.
The CustomerID <> predicate can scan a broad portion of Sales.Orders.
CustomerID is compared to a hard-coded business identifier.
The CROSS JOIN multiplies every Orders row by every Customers row.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned joined row is arbitrary.
The comma-separated table list uses old-style join syntax.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned order is arbitrary.
The scalar subquery counts order lines separately for each outer order row.
The correlated EXISTS subquery is evaluated against Sales.OrderLines for each outer order.
The CASE expression used to calculate HasComments is repeated in ORDER BY.
ORDER BY 2 depends on select-list position and becomes fragile when the projection changes.
Pagination orders only by OrderDate, which is not a stable unique tie-breaker.
The branches filter on mutually exclusive OrderID values, so UNION duplicate elimination is unnecessary.
OrderID is compared to the hard-coded business identifier 1.
OrderID is compared to the hard-coded business identifier 2.
COUNT(*) > 0 counts all matching order lines when an EXISTS predicate is sufficient.
SELECT variable assignment silently accepts multiple rows and retains an arbitrary last value.
The table variable can receive poor cardinality estimates compared with a temporary table.
TOP (5) has no ORDER BY, so the inserted OrderID values are arbitrary.
SELECT INTO #ImplicitOrders creates the temporary-table schema implicitly.
TOP (5) has no ORDER BY, so the rows copied to #ImplicitOrders are arbitrary.
The cursor processes query results row by row instead of using a set-based operation.
The cursor query uses TOP (2) without ORDER BY, producing arbitrary rows.
The WHILE loop performs iterative processing rather than a set-based operation.
Dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating a variable value.
The dynamic SQL is executed without parameterized inputs.
Dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating a variable value.
sp_executesql is called without binding values through a parameter definition.
#Scratch is explicitly created but never dropped before the procedure ends.
The CATCH block returns an error message but does not throw, raise an error, or return a failure status.
RAISERROR is used for a new error path instead of THROW.
PRINT is buffered and unsuitable for structured diagnostic reporting.
CustomerID is compared to a hard-coded business identifier.
The date constant 20160101 is embedded directly in query logic without a named parameter.
The date string 01/02/2016 is converted without an explicit style and can be interpreted differently by session settings.
COALESCE is applied to both join keys, which can inhibit normal join optimization.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned order is arbitrary.
Sales.Orders is self-joined only to return OrderID already available from the first alias.
TOP (1) has no ORDER BY, so the returned order is arbitrary.
Sorting by the nvarchar(max) Comments column can require a wide and expensive sort.
SUM aggregates all Sales.OrderLines rows without a restrictive filter.
The row-level OrderID > 0 predicate is placed in HAVING instead of WHERE.
COUNT(*) without GROUP BY returns one row, so ORDER BY is unnecessary.
MERGE has documented concurrency and correctness edge cases.
The MERGE update assigns Comments to its existing value, causing unnecessary writes when matched.
Comments is assigned to itself, causing logging and locking without changing data.
The DELETE filters only by Quantity = -1, which is a broad non-key predicate.
The DELETE uses the magic sentinel value -1 for Quantity.
WAITFOR DELAY blocks a SQL Server worker intentionally.
BEGIN TRANSACTION is outside a TRY/CATCH error-handling boundary.
SET IDENTITY_INSERT ON changes session state and creates fragile identity-management coupling.
sp_who is executed without an explicit schema qualifier.
SET ROWCOUNT changes session behavior and is not restored before the procedure ends.
INSERT INTO #DemoCategories uses VALUES without an explicit target column list.
The inserted CategoryID value 99 is a hard-coded business identifier.
The constructed LIKE pattern starts with %, preventing a normal index seek on CustomerName.
Dynamic SQL text is built by concatenating @CustomerName.
The dynamic SQL constructs a LIKE pattern beginning with %.
User-supplied @CustomerName is concatenated directly into executable SQL text.
sp_executesql executes the dynamic statement without parameterized inputs.
sp_executesql is invoked without an explicit schema qualifier.
The UPDATE on #DemoCustomers has no WHERE clause and updates every row.
CustomerCategoryID is assigned the hard-coded business identifier 1.
The WHERE predicate on outer-joined alias o removes NULL-extended rows, effectively converting the LEFT JOIN to an inner join.
The date constant 2016-01-01 is embedded directly in query logic.
DeliveryMethodID = NULL never evaluates to true; use IS NULL instead.
dbo.GetCustomerType is a scalar UDF in the SELECT list and can force row-by-row evaluation.
The CATCH block returns an error message but does not throw, raise an error, or return a failure status.
The CATCH block handles errors from an explicit transaction without rolling it back.
The hard-coded INDEX = 1 hint couples the query plan to a specific index choice.
The query contains four nested derived-table levels and should be refactored for readability and optimization.
The LIKE pattern starts with %, preventing a normal index seek on CustomerName.
SELECT * from dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti returns every function column unnecessarily.
dbo.GetCustomerOrdersMulti appears to be a multi-statement table-valued function, which can have poor cardinality estimates compared with an inline TVF.
